text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to begin the hepatectomy, with the normal liver visible on the left side of the screen and the tumor attached to it. The vessel sealer is employed to divide adhesions between the tumor and the omentum, freeing the tumor. The liver, showing fatty changes, appears more yellow than usual. The surgeon uses hook cautery to take down attachments between the liver and diaphragm, allowing for better mobility to safely remove the tumor.

The surgeon identifies the normal liver on the left side of the screen and the tumor growing from it. A scoring line is marked on the liver to delineate the area for division, ensuring complete removal of the tumor.

The surgeon uses a harmonic HD scalpel to begin dividing the scored portion of the liver, aiming to minimize bleeding due to the liver's vascular nature. The harmonic scalpel is applied to the liver's surface and the initial centimeters of tissue, while larger blood vessels deeper in the liver will require a stapler for division.

The surgeon methodically divides the liver parenchyma, starting at one end and progressing in a shallow manner across the tissue. Upon reaching the end, the process is repeated from the beginning, gradually deepening the dissection.

The surgeon has divided the liver parenchyma and approaches the large blood vessels that require management with a vascular stapler. The stapler articulates to bend in various directions, allowing precise angling to divide the tissue. Multiple firings may be necessary to fully free the specimen.

The surgeon completes the final firing of the vascular stapler, fully freeing the liver specimen. The cut surface of the liver is visible, and the surgeon uses an argon beam to cauterize the edge, sealing exposed blood vessels and bile ducts to prevent postoperative bleeding and bile leaks. The specimen is placed in a retrieval bag for removal and sent to pathology for margin confirmation, completing the partial hepatectomy.
